title: Linked Lists
description: An in-depth look at Singly and Doubly Linked Lists in Python, covering pointer traversals, node manipulation, and reference models.

sections.title: Singly Linked List
sections.description: A Singly Linked List is a linear data structure where each node points to the next node in the sequence. It allows efficient insertion and deletion at head, but requires O(N) time to search or access arbitrary elements.
sections.codeBlock:
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

# Helper to print linked list
def print_list(head):
    current = head
    path = []
    while current:
        path.append(str(current.val))
        current = current.next
    print(" -> ".join(path))

# Constructing: 10 -> 20 -> 30
head = ListNode(10)
head.next = ListNode(20)
head.next.next = ListNode(30)

print_list(head)       # 10 -> 20 -> 30

sections.title: Doubly Linked List
sections.description: A Doubly Linked List nodes contain references to both the next and the previous nodes. This enables bi-directional traversal and O(1) deletions if the node pointer is known.
sections.codeBlock:
class DoublyListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, prev=None, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.prev = prev
        self.next = next

# Constructing: 100 <-> 200
node1 = DoublyListNode(100)
node2 = DoublyListNode(200)
node1.next = node2
node2.prev = node1

print(node2.prev.val)  # 100
print(node1.next.val)  # 200

details:
Memory Fragmentation: Unlike arrays/lists which are allocated in contiguous blocks of memory, linked list nodes are dynamically instantiated at arbitrary memory locations. Pointers/references link these objects together. This eliminates the need for expensive memory reallocations or shifts, but introduces overhead (each node requires additional bytes to store reference addresses) and results in poor CPU cache locality.

Complexity Analysis: Insertion and deletion operations at the beginning of a Singly Linked List take O(1) time. However, deleting an arbitrary node requires traversing the preceding nodes, taking O(N) time. A Doubly Linked List optimizes this by allowing direct O(1) removal of any node if we already hold its reference, at the cost of doubling the pointer memory requirements.
